summary: Stancil reviews a decade of data-industry predictions and finds that fewer than half were mostly correct, with many being vague, trend‑extrapolation filler. He argues that forecasts repeatedly promise imminent breakthroughs (AI, predictive analytics, streaming, data marketplaces) that arrive later or not at all, while genuinely important developments often go unpredicted. The upshot: expect 2023 to miss in familiar ways, with surprises filling the gaps.
money_quotes: In total, I’d estimate that less than half of the nearly 600 predictions issued by various experts over the last ten years could be reasonably scored as correct.
We stubbornly predict, year after year, that this is finally the moment some sweeping change bears fruit.
To adapt Rudi Dornbusch’s famous quote about economics, “things take longer to happen than you think they will, and then they happen faster than you thought they could.”
Outside of a few fuzzy nods towards the emergence of a new analytics stack, we didn’t anticipate the explosion of the modern data stack.
If past performance is any indicator of future results, 2023 won’t look like we expect.
key_insight: A decade of data forecasts shows a pattern of over‑eager, vague predictions that miss timing, while the biggest shifts often emerge unexpectedly.
